notes:
- Key concepts introduced (with brief explanation)
  - Termination conditions: rules that stop a running Autogen team — can be predefined or custom (e.g., a function named termination or a text match like "approve").
  - Custom termination function: you can provide a function (e.g., final greeting message triggers) to programmatically terminate execution.
  - Round-robin team treated like any other team: round-robin teams can be controlled the same way as other teams.
  - Human-in-the-loop (HITL): integrating a human into agent workflows so humans can provide feedback/control either during or after team runs.
  - User proxy agent: an agent that proxies/mimics the human and is used to inject human input during a running team (i.e., during execution).
  - Stateful teams / save-and-load state: teams can persist their state so after a run completes you can resume or continue the conversation without a user proxy.
  - Two HITL approaches:
    - During-run HITL via user proxy agent (blocking until human input supplied).
    - Post-run HITL via stateful team continuation (resume interaction after the run has finished).
  - Input function customization: you can plug any input function into the user proxy; the default Python input() is often used.
  - Integration with UI frameworks: can integrate Autogen agents with front-ends like Streamlit to collect human feedback in-app.
  - Autogen as a mature, programmable framework: emphasis that Autogen prefers programmatic control (vs drag-and-drop) and appeals to pro programmers who want to modify core behavior.

- Any analogies or metaphors used
  - Autogen behaves "like a very mature framework" (contrast vs drag-and-drop).
  - "Jump onto the core" — programmers can dive into internals and change code.
  - User sits "outside" the team/session and the user proxy agent "comes back to you" (visual diagram metaphor).
  - Teams "on hold" until human input — human input acts like a pause/resume control.

- Any code, commands, or config shown (captured from narration)
  - Imports referenced: async.io, UserProxyAgent, AssistantAgent, console, load_env (names as narrated).
  - Files referenced: agent.py, demo Python file showing input() usage (example named variations like 9, 9.0, 9.1 in narrative).
  - Using default Python input() as the user proxy input function.
  - Termination token/example: "approve" (termination defined as checking if text "approve" is present).
  - Example execution flow/commands (narrative versions):
    - Running Python script locally: python 9.0 (used to demonstrate input()); python 9.1 (used to run the Autogen demo).
    - Team run API variants mentioned in speech: team.run, team.run_stream / teamr runstream (narration not consistent but indicates functions to run teams/streams).
  - Example prompt used in demo: "write a nice fourline poem about India" and follow-ups like "tell something about its history as well" and "approve".
  - Example output snippet quoted in narration: "India shines with color bright and sand" (assistant-generated poem line).
  - Example simple Python input demo code described:
    - print("hello world")
    - input("how are you")
    - print("by world")
  - Mention of "agent_py" (agent.py) as a provided example in documentation.

- Any warnings, gotchas, or important caveats
  - Termination checking occurs after every message — if the termination token/text appears in any message, the team will stop.
  - When using user-proxy during a run, the team will block/wait ("on hold") until the human supplies input.
  - ChatGPT-style chat does not normally ask the user mid-response; the during-run user-proxy behavior is different and not available in standard ChatGPT flows.
  - Potential import/name errors: if you ask the AI to generate code it might incorrectly choose import names (speaker cautions about AI making an error using "pi autogen" or wrong module names); ensure correct imports (e.g., agents vs autogen).
  - Environment/kernel issues: speaker experienced kernel selection and dashboard problems when running demos (be prepared to select the correct kernel and handle missing dashboards).
  - Diagrams/explanations may be tricky to understand at first — user proxy flow diagrams can be non-intuitive.

- Any comparisons or lists (potential table candidates)
  - Two ways to include humans in agent workflows:
    - During-run (User Proxy Agent)
      - When: while team execution is ongoing
      - How: insert a user-proxy agent that requests input and returns it to the team
      - Behavior: blocking/waits for human input
      - Use-case: mid-flow corrections, approvals, formatting checks
    - Post-run (Stateful continuation)
      - When: after team run completes
      - How: load saved team state and send additional messages
      - Behavior: non-blocking continuation similar to ChatGPT multi-turn
      - Use-case: iterative follow-ups, extending or modifying previously-computed outputs
  - Customer support example agents:
    - General query agent
    - Refund agent
    - Order/booking agent
    - Selector/group chat to route to human customer care (human in the loop)
  - Team participants in demo:
    - AssistantAgent (simple assistant)
    - UserProxyAgent (proxies the user/human)

- Direct quotes worth preserving
  - "termination condition"
  - "approve" (as the termination token used in the demo)
  - "user proxy agent"
  - "human in the loop"
  - "Autogen has behaved like a very mature framework where you can control the things as much as you want"
  - "if you are a pro programmer you will love this thing a lot about autogen because you can jump onto the core"
  - Example assistant output line: "India shines with color bright and sand"
  - Example input prompt used in demo: "write a nice fourline poem about India"
